description: MITRE ATT&CK rules for browser forensics (phishing URL detection). These rules analyze a case-level aggregate feature vector built from predict_browser_case() output — phishing counts, high-risk counts, login-credential exposure, and drive-by download exposure across all URLs found in browser history/BFT/PassView.

rules.description: Detects that at least one URL in the browser history was classified as phishing by the rule pre-check layer or the trained RandomForest model. A visited phishing link is itself evidence the user was targeted by a social-engineering delivery attempt, regardless of whether further compromise occurred.
rules.recommendation: Identify how the phishing link was delivered (email, chat, ad) by correlating browser history timestamps with mail/messaging logs. Warn the user and check whether the link was clicked from a corporate device on the network. Add the domain to the organization's blocklist.

rules.description: Detects that the user actively navigated to (executed) a phishing link rather than the link merely appearing in a log passively — browser history entries represent completed navigation, meaning the user's action delivered the payload/landing page.
rules.recommendation: Determine what the phishing page prompted the user to do (enter credentials, download a file, run a script) by revisiting the page content/screenshot if preserved. Provide targeted user awareness training referencing this specific incident.

rules.description: Detects that a saved password (from WebBrowserPassView) corresponds to a URL classified as phishing — meaning the user's credentials were likely entered into and captured by an attacker-controlled login page. This is the single strongest indicator in browser forensics: a confirmed credential exposure, not just a suspicious visit.
rules.mitre_technique_name: Credentials from Password Stores: Credentials from Web Browsers
rules.recommendation: Treat as a confirmed credential compromise: force an immediate password reset for every account that used this saved login, and enable multi-factor authentication. Review authentication logs for the affected account(s) for any sign-ins from unfamiliar locations/devices since the phishing visit. Preserve the PassView export and browser history as evidence before any remediation.

rules.description: Detects that a file was downloaded from a URL also classified as phishing, consistent with a drive-by-compromise delivery mechanism where the phishing page itself serves a malicious payload rather than (or in addition to) harvesting credentials.
rules.recommendation: Locate the downloaded file on disk (correlate download timestamp with the disk/malware AI pipelines) and submit it for static/dynamic malware analysis immediately. Treat the host as potentially compromised pending that analysis — isolate if the file was executed. Cross-reference this URL and file hash with the malware AI pipeline's findings for the same host.

rules.description: Fires when the proportion of phishing URLs relative to total browsing history is unusually high, suggesting the user's browsing activity was concentrated in a phishing campaign window (e.g. clicked multiple links from the same campaign) rather than a single isolated incident.
rules.recommendation: Review the full browsing timeline around the flagged URLs to identify the phishing campaign's entry point (email link, malvertising, compromised site redirect). Check other users/hosts in the organization for visits to the same domains — this rate suggests a targeted or widespread campaign, not an isolated click.

rules.description: Detects that more than two URLs were independently classified as HIGH risk, indicating a pattern of risky browsing behavior rather than a single incidental phishing hit — worth investigating as a broader exposure window.
rules.recommendation: Enumerate every HIGH-risk URL and its visit timestamp to build a timeline of the exposure window. Check whether these URLs share a common referrer, ad network, or delivery vector. Recommend browser-level protections (Safe Browsing enforcement, DNS filtering) going forward.

rules.rule_name: Composite Critical Exposure — Stolen Credentials at Scale
rules.description: Escalates to the highest severity when credential exposure (login_phishing_count) co-occurs with a broader pattern of phishing visits (three or more), indicating the credential theft was not an isolated one-off but part of a sustained campaign the user was repeatedly exposed to — raising the likelihood that the stolen credentials are already being used by an attacker.
rules.recommendation: Escalate immediately to full incident-response: assume the exposed credentials may already be in use by an attacker (valid-account abuse). Reset passwords, revoke active sessions/tokens, enable MFA, and audit account activity across all services using this credential (password reuse check). Notify the account owner and, if a corporate account, security leadership.
